It was there that "my lord Faruskiar ... had joined the train at Douchak so as to accompany the treasure to its destination," (ASC-018-006) and there that the reporter noticed "the suspicious-looking Mongols we picked up at Douchak." (ASC-011-017) The distinction is later drawn between the ordinary passengers and "those we picked up at Douchak when we picked up Faruskiar and Ghangir." (ASC-019-003)

The river's chief role was as one arm of the twofold Patagonian watercourse, forever named beside the Rio Negro, whose parallel crossings framed the whole search along the thirty-seventh degree. (SC-010-033)
label: Raleigh

The Grand Transasiatic train had been switched onto the Nanking branch, running at tremendous speed toward the valley of the Tjon, which was spanned only by an unfinished viaduct three hundred and fifty to four hundred feet long; had the train been hurled to the bottom of that chasm, a hundred victims would have perished (ASC-025-003).

Why he "had hidden himself in the Falklands under the name of Hunt" (AM-017-135) becomes the question that Jeorling presses upon him directly, asking "what it was that induced you to reveal it" (AM-018-127).
# Claudius Bombarnac
Erik — "Erik Hersebom!" (WC-001-026) — is the adopted son of a fisher family of Noroe, on the Norwegian coast, and the gifted child at the heart of the novel.
